skill.name: molecular-review-workflow
skill.description: Generates academic reviews for molecules in diseases using PubMed research. Invoke when user needs biomedical literature review with Vancouver citation format.
skill.summary: Automated biomedical literature review generator using PubMed with Vancouver-style citations.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Evaluated 38 static findings across 4 files (583 lines). All CRITICAL and HIGH findings are false positives: the heuristic combination flag reflects the skill's legitimate PubMed search design, 'weak cryptographic algorithm' flags hit argparse docstrings and plain text, and 'external_commands' flags are markdown code formatting (backticks). The skill's env_access (NCBI API credentials), network (PubMed Entrez API), and script execution are transparent, well-documented, and serve the declared purpose. No prompt injection, obfuscation, or malicious intent detected. Minor residual risk from environment variable credential handling is acceptable given NCBI API keys are for rate-limiting, not secrets.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Access for NCBI API Credentials
low_findings.description: The script reads NCBI_EMAIL and NCBI_API_KEY from environment variables at pubmed_api.py lines 20-21. This is documented and expected for NCBI Entrez API access. NCBI API keys are free rate-limiting tokens, not sensitive secrets. The skill transparency documents this requirement in SKILL.md lines 84-88.

low_findings.title: Command-Line Argument Input in Test Block
low_findings.description: The __main__ block at pubmed_api.py line 99 accepts a search query via sys.argv. This is a test/debug entry point and the input is passed to the Entrez API which handles sanitization. Not exploitable in the skill's normal execution context.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Code Fences Misidentified as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged 16 markdown code blocks (```) and inline backticks in SKILL.md as Ruby/shell backtick execution operators. These are standard markdown formatting for documentation, not executable shell commands. All 16 instances are false positives.

dangerous_patterns.title: Argparse Docstring Misidentified as Weak Cryptography
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged argparse.ArgumentParser(description=...) calls in validate_skill.py line 12 and descriptive text strings in SKILL.md as 'weak cryptographic algorithm'. These contain no cryptographic operations whatsoever.

dangerous_patterns.title: Legitimate API Credential Pattern Misidentified as Secret Leakage
dangerous_patterns.description: The static analyzer flagged function parameter named 'api_key' in pubmed_api.py line 5 and its docstring at line 13, plus os.environ.get() calls at lines 20-21, as HIGH-severity credential access. The parameter definition and docstring are not credential access. The env var reads are standard practice for NCBI API configuration and the skill transparently documents this requirement.

content.user_title: Generate Academic Biomedical Literature Reviews with PubMed
content.value_statement: Researchers waste hours manually searching PubMed and formatting citations for literature reviews. This skill automates academic review generation with Vancouver-style citations for any molecule-disease combination using the NCBI PubMed database.
actual_capabilities: Searches PubMed for peer-reviewed articles on specified molecule-disease pairs
Generates structured academic reviews with Vancouver-style numbered citations
Translates non-English disease and molecule names to English for query construction
Produces deterministic, reproducible output with explicit assumptions documented
Validates inputs before execution and surfaces missing requirements clearly
Formats references with sequential citation numbering matching the reference list
limitations: Requires NCBI API credentials (email and API key) configured as environment variables
Depends on the Biopython library being installed in the execution environment
Review quality depends on available PubMed literature for the given molecule-disease pair
Does not perform systematic review or meta-analysis methodology

use_cases.title: Biomedical Researcher Drafting a Literature Review
use_cases.description: A researcher needs a quick structured review of how a specific molecule affects a disease. They provide the molecule and disease names and receive a formatted review with Vancouver citations ready for manuscript integration.

use_cases.title: Clinical Writer Preparing Background Sections
use_cases.description: A medical writer needs evidence-based background material on drug mechanisms. The skill searches PubMed and produces a structured summary with properly numbered references suitable for grant proposals or clinical documents.
use_cases.target_user: Medical writers and clinical research coordinators

use_cases.title: Student Learning Evidence-Based Review Methods
use_cases.description: A graduate student learning academic writing uses the skill to see how literature reviews are structured. The transparent workflow and deterministic output help them understand the review generation process and Vancouver citation formatting.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Molecule-Disease Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a literature review for the molecule metformin in the disease type 2 diabetes. Use PubMed to find relevant articles and format citations in Vancouver style.
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner: Simple single molecule-disease pair review

prompt_templates.title: Review with Translated Inputs
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a literature review for the molecule 'Resveratrol' in the disease 'cáncer de mama'. Translate non-English terms to English before searching PubMed, then produce a Vancouver-formatted review covering the molecule's role in the disease.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate: Input requires language translation before PubMed search

prompt_templates.title: Focused Mechanism Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate an academic review on the molecular mechanisms of curcumin in Alzheimer's disease. Search PubMed for articles from the last 5 years, focus on mechanistic pathways, and format all citations in Vancouver style with sequential numbering.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced: Specific mechanistic focus with recency constraints

prompt_templates.title: Comparative Multi-Molecule Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a comparative literature review for the molecules metformin, pioglitazone, and empagliflozin in type 2 diabetes. For each molecule, summarize its mechanism, clinical evidence, and safety profile using PubMed sources. Format all citations in Vancouver style with a single continuous reference list.
prompt_templates.scenario: Expert: Multi-molecule comparison with unified reference list

output_examples.input: Generate a literature review for metformin in type 2 diabetes
output_examples.output: A structured academic review with sections covering introduction, molecular mechanisms, clinical evidence, and conclusions. Each claim is supported by sequentially numbered Vancouver-style citations [1], [2], [3] matching the reference list at the end.

output_examples.input: Review the role of vitamin D in COVID-19 using PubMed
output_examples.output: A formatted review document with PubMed-sourced evidence on vitamin D immunomodulatory effects in COVID-19. The output includes a reference list with PMIDs, journal names, publication years, and proper Vancouver numbering synchronized with in-text citations.

best_practices: Set NCBI_EMAIL and NCBI_API_KEY environment variables before invoking the skill to avoid rate limiting
Provide specific molecule and disease names to get focused, relevant PubMed search results
Review generated output for accuracy and verify key citations against original PubMed articles before use
anti_patterns: Do not use the generated review as final publication-ready text without human verification of citations and claims
Do not invoke this skill when PubMed credentials are missing or the Biopython library is not installed
Do not expect systematic review quality from a single-pass automated PubMed search workflow

faq.question: What credentials do I need to use this skill?
faq.answer: You need an NCBI account with an email and API key. Set them as NCBI_EMAIL and NCBI_API_KEY environment variables. NCBI API keys are free and available from the NCBI account settings page.

faq.question: Does this skill work offline?
faq.answer: No. The skill requires network access to query the NCBI PubMed Entrez API. You must have internet connectivity and valid NCBI credentials.

faq.question: Can I use non-English disease or molecule names?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow includes an input translation step that translates disease and molecule names to English before constructing PubMed search queries.

faq.question: What citation format does this skill use?
faq.answer: The skill uses Vancouver citation format with sequentially numbered references. Citation numbers start from 1, increment through the text, and must match the reference list entries.

faq.question: How many PubMed articles does each review include?
faq.answer: By default, the script retrieves up to 5 articles per search query. You can adjust the max_results parameter in the Python script if you need more or fewer results.

faq.question: Is the generated review ready for publication?
faq.answer: No. The generated review is a starting point that requires human verification. You should check citation accuracy, verify claims against original articles, and edit the text to meet your publication standards.
